sections.header: Verses 1–13 — "Accept One Another as Christ Accepted You"

heb.gloss: to welcome / to accept / to receive
heb.paragraph: Proslambanō (v. 7) is the climactic command of the strong/weak discussion: ‘accept one another, then, just as Christ accepted you.’ The verb carries the sense of warm, personal welcome into fellowship. Christ’s acceptance of sinners is the model and motivation for mutual acceptance within the church. The purpose clause (‘for God’s glory’) elevates unity from a pastoral preference to a doxological imperative.

refs.note: Christ’s self-emptying as the model for Christian humility and other-regarding love.
The Root of Jesse will stand as a banner for the peoples — Paul cites this as the prophetic ground for Gentile hope.
Praise the LORD, all you nations — the shortest psalm, cited as evidence of God’s plan for Gentile worship.

notes.note: The strong ought to bear the failings of the weak and not please themselves. Christ is the supreme example: he bore reproach that was not his own (Ps 69:9).
Accept one another as Christ accepted you. This is the final application of the strong/weak discussion: Christlike acceptance is the hallmark of authentic Christian community.

Christ became a servant to the circumcised (confirming the patriarchal promises) so that the Gentiles might glorify God for his mercy. The four OT quotations prove that Gentile inclusion was prophetically anticipated.
The God of hope fills believers with joy and peace through believing, so that they overflow with hope by the power of the Holy Spirit. Hope is the final word of the letter’s theological argument.
Calvin observes that Christ’s example is both the model and the motive: we bear the weaknesses of others because Christ bore ours. Self-denial is not stoic discipline but Christlike love.
Calvin sees mutual acceptance as the practical test of whether a church has grasped the gospel. Where the gospel is truly understood, judgmental exclusion gives way to generous welcome.

The statement that ‘everything written in the past was written to teach us’ establishes a hermeneutical principle: the OT remains instructive for the church, even though its ceremonial demands have been fulfilled in Christ.
The Isaiah 11:10 quotation (‘the Root of Jesse’) is messianic and eschatological. Paul applies it to Christ’s lordship over the nations.

Moo reads this section as the Christological summit of the strong/weak discussion. Christ’s self-sacrifice is the definitive argument for the strong to sacrifice their freedom for the weak. The OT catena then grounds the unity of Jewish and Gentile believers in the prophetic plan of God.

Schreiner emphasises that the call to ‘accept one another’ is not merely tolerant coexistence but active, welcoming inclusion modelled on Christ’s initiative. The Christological pattern (v. 8: Christ became a servant) and the eschatological vision (v. 12: Gentiles hoping in the Root of Jesse) together ground the ecclesiological imperative.

hist.context: Romans 15:1–13 concludes the strong/weak discussion begun in 14:1 with a Christological foundation: Christ did not please himself (v. 3) but bore the weaknesses of others. Paul’s catena of OT quotations (vv. 9–12, from 2 Sam 22:50, Deut 32:43, Ps 117:1, Isa 11:10) demonstrates that the inclusion of Gentiles alongside Jews was always God’s plan. The benediction of v. 13 (‘May the God of hope fill you with all joy and peace’) effectively closes the body of the letter. Everything that follows is personal and travelogue.

sections.header: Verses 14–33 — "Paul’s Plan to Visit Rome and Spain"

heb.paragraph: Leitourgos in v. 16 is a cultic term: Paul describes himself as a ‘priestly servant’ (leitourgon Christou Iēsou) of Christ to the Gentiles, administering the ‘priestly duty’ (hierourgounta) of the gospel. The Gentile converts are the ‘offering’ (prosphora) Paul presents to God. This sacerdotal language reframes missionary work as an act of worship.

refs.note: Paul resolves to go through Macedonia and Achaia to Jerusalem and then to Rome — the itinerary described here.
Paul’s extended treatment of the collection for the Jerusalem saints.
The arrest in Jerusalem that Paul feared and for which he requested prayer.

notes.note: Paul describes his mission in priestly terms: he is a minister of Christ Jesus to the Gentiles, performing the priestly duty of proclaiming the gospel. The converts are the offering, sanctified by the Spirit.
Paul has fully proclaimed the gospel from Jerusalem to Illyricum. His ambition is to preach where Christ has not been named, building on no other foundation (cf. 1 Cor 3:10).
Paul’s plan: deliver the collection to Jerusalem, then visit Rome en route to Spain. Rome is not the destination but a waystation for the mission to the western Mediterranean.
Paul’s request for prayer reveals his awareness that the Jerusalem visit is dangerous. He asks for deliverance from unbelievers in Judea and acceptance by the Jerusalem church.
Calvin admires Paul’s ambition to preach where Christ has not been named. This is the mark of a true apostle: pioneer mission, not ministry in established churches.
Calvin sees the collection as a practical expression of Jewish-Gentile unity. The Gentiles owe a spiritual debt to Israel; the collection is a tangible acknowledgement of that debt.
The reference to Illyricum (modern Albania/Dalmatia) marks the northwestern extent of Paul’s mission. Whether Paul actually entered Illyricum or merely reached its border is debated.
The mention of Spain (Spanian) is significant: it represents the farthest western frontier of the Roman Empire. Paul’s missionary vision is global.

Moo reads this section as a window into Paul’s missionary strategy and self-understanding. Paul sees himself as the apostle to the Gentiles par excellence, and his letter to Rome is not merely pastoral but strategic: he seeks the Roman church’s support for the Spanish mission.

Schreiner emphasises the collection as a theological statement: the sharing of material blessings from Gentiles to Jews demonstrates the one people of God across ethnic lines. The collection embodies the unity Paul has argued for throughout the letter.

hist.context: Romans 15:14–33 reveals Paul’s travel plans and missionary strategy. He has fully preached the gospel from Jerusalem to Illyricum (v. 19) and now plans to visit Rome en route to Spain (v. 24), the western end of the known world. First, however, he must deliver the collection to Jerusalem (vv. 25–27) — a project he views as a demonstration of Gentile-Jewish unity. The collection symbolises the Gentile churches’ gratitude for the spiritual blessings they received from Jewish Christians. Paul’s request for prayer (vv. 30–32) reveals his awareness of danger in Jerusalem, which was fulfilled when he was arrested there (Acts 21:27–36).

rows.label: vv.8–13
rows.text: Christ, Servant of Jews and Hope of Gentiles

rows.label: vv.14–21
rows.text: Paul’s Priestly Ministry to the Gentiles

lit.note: Romans 15 concludes the strong/weak discussion with Christ’s example (vv. 1–13) and reveals Paul’s missionary strategy (vv. 14–33). The letter’s theological argument ends at v. 13; what follows is personal and strategic.

themes.note: Mission peaks at 10: Paul’s travel plans, pioneer evangelism ambition, and priestly service language make this the most mission-focused chapter in Romans. Covenant and Mercy both at 7 reflect the unity themes.

Christ’s acceptance of sinners is the model and motivation for mutual acceptance within the church. The purpose clause (‘for God’s glory’) elevates unity from a pastoral preference to a doxological imperative.

Leitourgos in v. 16 is a cultic term: Paul describes himself as a ‘priestly servant’ (leitourgon Christou Iēsou) of Christ to the Gentiles, administering the ‘priestly duty’ (hierourgounta) of the gospel. ppl.name: Paul
ppl.role: Apostle to the Gentiles; author of thirteen NT letters
ppl.text: Paul (Saul of Tarsus) is introduced as a persecutor at Stephen’s stoning (Acts 7:58) and is dramatically converted on the Damascus road (Acts 9). He dominates Acts 13–28 across three missionary journeys, planting churches from Cyprus to Greece to Rome. His final journey to Rome as a prisoner frames Acts’ theological climax: the gospel reaching the empire’s capital, "boldly and without hindrance" (Acts 28:31).

trans.title: Comparing translations in Christ’s Example and Paul’s Mission

translations.text: and, moreover, that the Gentiles might glorify God for his mercy. As it is written: “Therefore I will praise you among the Gentiles; I will sing the praises of your name.”
and in order that the Gentiles might glorify God for his mercy. As it is written, “Therefore I will praise you among the Gentiles, and sing to your name.”
For everything that was written in the past was written to teach us, so that through the endurance taught in the Scriptures and the encouragement they provide we might have hope.
For whatever was written in former days was written for our instruction, that through endurance and through the encouragement of the Scriptures we might have hope.

Emphasises historical-critical, literary, or text-critical analysis.
Reads Christ’s Example and Paul’s Mission within the canonical framework of fulfilled prophecy and covenant theology.
Represented by Calvin, MacArthur, and the evangelical tradition.
Both readings engage the text seriously; they differ primarily in their hermeneutical starting points and assumptions about authorship and historical context.

thread.text: Christ’s self-emptying as the model for Christian humility and other-regarding love.
The Root of Jesse will stand as a banner for the peoples — Paul cites this as the prophetic ground for Gentile hope.
Praise the LORD, all you nations — the shortest psalm, cited as evidence of God’s plan for Gentile worship.
Paul resolves to go through Macedonia and Achaia to Jerusalem and then to Rome — the itinerary described here.
Paul’s extended treatment of the collection for the Jerusalem saints.
The arrest in Jerusalem that Paul feared and for which he requested prayer.

discourse.thesis: The strong ought to bear with the weak and not please themselves; Christ accepted us — accept one another to the glory of God.

nodes.text: We who are strong ought to bear with the failings of the weak and not to please ourselves. Let each of us please his neighbor for his good, to build him up. Christ did not please himself — 'The reproaches of those who reproached you fell on me.' Whatever was written was written for our instruction, that through endurance and encouragement of the Scriptures we might have hope.

children.text: May the God of endurance and encouragement grant you to live in harmony with one another, that together with one voice you may glorify the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ.

nodes.text: Welcome one another as Christ has welcomed you, for the glory of God. Christ became a servant to the circumcised to show God's truthfulness and to confirm the promises to the patriarchs, and in order that the Gentiles might glorify God for his mercy. As it is written: 'I will praise you among the Gentiles' — and again, 'Rejoice, O Gentiles, with his people' — and again, 'Praise the Lord, all you Gentiles' — and Isaiah says, 'The root of Jesse will come, and he will arise to rule the Gentiles; in him will the Gentiles hope.'

children.text: May the God of hope fill you with all joy and peace in believing, so that by the power of the Holy Spirit you may abound in hope.

nodes.text: Paul is satisfied the Romans are full of goodness and knowledge, able to instruct one another. He writes boldly because of the grace given him to be a minister of Christ Jesus to the Gentiles — a priestly service so that the offering of the Gentiles may be acceptable. In Christ Jesus he has reason to boast in what pertains to God. He speaks only of what Christ has accomplished through him — by word and deed, by signs and wonders, by the power of the Spirit — to bring the Gentiles to obedience. From Jerusalem to Illyricum he has fully proclaimed the gospel, making it his ambition to preach where Christ has not been named.

children.text: Paul's pioneer principle: not building on another's foundation. As Isaiah wrote: 'Those who have never been told of him will see; those who have never heard will understand.'

nodes.text: This is why Paul has often been hindered from coming to Rome. But now, with no more room in these regions, he longs to see them on his way to Spain and to be helped on his journey by them. First he must go to Jerusalem with aid for the saints — Macedonia and Achaia have contributed for the poor among the saints in Jerusalem. They were pleased to do it, and indeed they owe it to them: if the Gentiles have shared in their spiritual blessings, they ought to serve them in material blessings. When he completes this and delivers the fruit, he will visit them en route to Spain.

Paul urges them by the Lord Jesus Christ and the love of the Spirit to strive together with him in prayers to God on his behalf — that he may be delivered from the unbelievers in Judea, that his service may be acceptable to the saints, and that he may come to them with joy and be refreshed in their company. May the God of peace be with you all. Amen.

discourse.note: Romans 15 reveals Paul's travel plans: Jerusalem collection → Rome → Spain. The Spain mission places Rome as his staging base, explaining why he writes to a church he did not found. The catena of OT texts (vv. 9-12) grounds Gentile inclusion in Scripture.

coaching.tip: 'We who are strong have an obligation to bear with the failings of the weak' (v. 1). Strength obligates service. Christ 'did not please himself' (v. 3) — he's the model. The strong accommodate the weak, not vice versa. This inverts the world's power dynamics. Christian strength serves weakness.

'Christ became a servant to the circumcised... in order that the Gentiles might glorify God for his mercy' (vv. 8-9). Paul's mission to Gentiles flows from Christ's mission to Israel. The particular (Israel) opens to the universal (nations). Old Testament quotations from Torah, Prophets, and Writings confirm Gentile inclusion. Paul's work continues Christ's.
